key_insight: Local redundancy is a theoretically grounded information-theoretic measure of neural-network plasticity, and it can be approximated by the expected squared gradient norm on a synthetic memorization task.
engineering_relevance: For model selection in continual and transfer learning, this offers a more reliable checkpoint/plasticity indicator than common heuristics like effective rank, dead neuron fraction, or weight norm.
method: Define plasticity via the worst-case redundancy of a local model family around gradient-direction perturbations.
Use universal compression theory to formalize local redundancy as an information-theoretic quantity.
Derive the expected squared gradient norm on a synthetic memorization task as a computable lower bound.
evidence_signal: Across continual image classification and time-series transfer learning, the proposed measure predicts downstream performance better than existing metrics and can select pretraining checkpoints even when validation loss has plateaued.
watch_item: Exact local redundancy is intractable, so the practical proxy depends on the synthetic memorization setup and gradient estimates, which may add computation and sensitivity to task design.
paper_title: Local Redundancy: An Information-Theoretic Measure of Plasticity from Synthetic Memorization
